notes: Verification pass for ch.26. searches: ~7, clusters: 5 (A: Sabbath changed by papal/Roman power; B: continuity of Sabbath-keeping / Seventh Day Baptists; C: 2300-days=1844 computation; D: post-1844 Adventist date-setting & Sabbatarian controversy; plus ~19 KJV scripture quote-fidelity checks handled via common-knowledge fast-path). Local academic sources used: Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday ch.6 (Gregorian UP 1977); period baseline: Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873). Control sample of needs_verification:false claims: GC-26-011, GC-26-041, GC-26-091 (all found to carry checkable kernels that hold). Escalation applied to Sabbath-history and 1844 clusters (source_quality noted per verdict).

claims.quote: The work of Sabbath reform to be accomplished in the last days is foretold in the prophecy of Isaiah
claims.claim_text: A future work of Sabbath reform to be accomplished in the last days is foretold in the book of Isaiah.

verification.rationale: Theological/prophetic-application claim (that Isaiah foretells a last-days Sabbath reform). Not empirically checkable; logged for centrality analysis.

claims.quote: “Thus saith the Lord, Keep ye judgment, and do justice: for My salvation is near to come, and My righteousness to be revealed. Blessed is the man that doeth this, and the son of man that layeth hold on it; that keepeth the Sabbath from polluting it, and keepeth his hand from doing any evil.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the passage beginning "Thus saith the Lord, Keep ye judgment, and do justice... that keepeth the Sabbath from polluting it, and keepeth his hand from doing any evil" (cf. Isaiah 56:1-2).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 56:1-2 (pronoun capitalization per GC house style). Matches the standard text.

claims.quote: “The sons of the stranger, that join themselves to the Lord, to serve Him, and to love the name of the Lord, to be His servants, every one that keepeth the Sabbath from polluting it, and taketh hold of My covenant; even them will I bring to My holy mountain, and make them joyful in My house of prayer.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the passage "The sons of the stranger, that join themselves to the Lord... even them will I bring to My holy mountain, and make them joyful in My house of prayer" (cf. Isaiah 56:6-7).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 56:6-7 (opening word "Also" dropped as the quote begins mid-verse). Text and sense unaltered.

claims.quote: These words apply in the Christian age, as shown by the context
claims.claim_text: The Isaiah 56 Sabbath passage applies specifically to the Christian age.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/theological application of an Isaiah passage; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: “The Lord God which gathereth the outcasts of Israel saith, Yet will I gather others to him, beside those that are gathered unto him.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the statement "The Lord God which gathereth the outcasts of Israel saith, Yet will I gather others to him, beside those that are gathered unto him" (cf. Isaiah 56:8).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 56:8.

claims.quote: Here is foreshadowed the gathering in of the Gentiles by the gospel.
claims.claim_text: Isaiah 56:8 foreshadows the gathering in of the Gentiles by the gospel.

verification.rationale: Theological interpretation of a prophetic verse; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: And upon those who then honor the Sabbath, a blessing is pronounced.
claims.claim_text: A blessing is pronounced in Isaiah upon those who honor the Sabbath during the Christian age.

verification.rationale: Theological application of Isaiah 56; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: Thus the obligation of the fourth commandment extends past the crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension of Christ, to the time when His servants should preach to all nations the message of glad tidings.
claims.claim_text: The obligation of the fourth commandment (the Sabbath) extends past the crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension of Christ into the era of gospel proclamation to all nations.

verification.rationale: Theological/doctrinal claim about the continuing obligation of the Sabbath; a matter of religious interpretation, not empirical verification.

claims.quote: The Lord commands by the same prophet, “Bind up the testimony, seal the law among My disciples.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the command "Bind up the testimony, seal the law among My disciples" (cf. Isaiah 8:16).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 8:16.

claims.quote: The seal of God’s law is found in the fourth commandment.
claims.claim_text: The "seal" of God's law referenced in Isaiah 8:16 is found specifically in the fourth commandment.

verification.rationale: Doctrinal interpretation (the "seal" motif applied to the Sabbath commandment); not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: This only, of all the ten, brings to view both the name and the title of the Lawgiver.
claims.claim_text: Of the Ten Commandments, only the fourth commandment names both the identity and the title of the Lawgiver (God).
claims.extractor_note: Borderline: this is a claim about the textual content of the Decalogue (checkable by reading Exodus 20), but is presented here as a theological/doctrinal argument about the Sabbath's function as a 'seal.' Logged as interpretive per instructions for sweeping doctrinal argument; flagging for possible reclassification.

verification.rationale: Control-sample check of a needs_verification:false claim that smuggles a checkable textual assertion about Exodus 20. The kernel holds: the divine name ("the LORD thy God") recurs in several commandments, but only the fourth commandment (Ex 20:8-11) supplies the Lawgiver's title and basis of authority — Creator of "heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is." Thus only the fourth couples name and title/office together. The wider theological inference (that this makes it the law's "seal") remains interpretive and is not adjudicated here.

sources.title: KJV Exodus 20:2-17 (Decalogue text)
sources.note: Only the fourth commandment identifies God as Creator of heaven and earth.

claims.quote: It declares Him to be the Creator of the heavens and the earth, and thus shows His claim to reverence and worship above all others.
claims.claim_text: The fourth commandment declares God to be the Creator of the heavens and the earth, showing His claim to reverence above all others.

verification.rationale: The textual kernel (the fourth commandment cites God as Creator, Ex 20:11) is accurate, but the framed claim is a doctrinal argument about worship-worthiness; treated as theological/interpretive.

claims.quote: Aside from this precept, there is nothing in the decalogue to show by whose authority the law is given.
claims.claim_text: Aside from the fourth commandment, nothing else in the Ten Commandments identifies by whose authority the law is given.

verification.rationale: Interpretive doctrinal claim closely tied to GC-26-011. Debatable on its face (the Decalogue's preamble, Ex 20:2, grounds the law in "the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt"), so the assertion depends on excluding the preamble from "the decalogue" proper — an interpretive move rather than a plain textual fact. Not adjudicated as true/false.

claims.quote: When the Sabbath was changed by the papal power, the seal was taken from the law.
claims.claim_text: The papacy changed the Sabbath (from the seventh day to Sunday), thereby removing the 'seal' from God's law.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-full. The causal claim that the Sabbath was changed "by the papal power" is genuinely disputed among modern scholars. Bacchiocchi (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press) argues the Church of Rome played a decisive leadership role in the 2nd-century promotion of Sunday and belittling of the Sabbath (anti-Judaic motive), which supports the causal direction White asserts. Mainstream scholarship, however, holds the shift was a gradual, regionally variable process across the 2nd-4th centuries (with Constantine's 321 civil law and centuries of dual observance), not a discrete decree of "papal power" — a framing anachronistic for the pre-Nicene period. The theological "seal" corollary is non-empirical.
verification.modern_consensus: Sunday's rise was gradual and multi-causal; a developed "papacy" did not yet exist in the 2nd century when Sunday worship spread. Rome's influence is affirmed by some (Bacchiocchi) but a single papal act is rejected.

sources.title: S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday, ch.6 'The Church of Rome and the Sabbath' (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press, 1977)
sources.note: Argues the Church of Rome led measures to wean Christians off Sabbath veneration in the 2nd c.; supports a Roman-church role but locates it before any developed papacy.

sources.title: When Did Sunday Become the Primary Christian Day of Worship (fact-check summary of scholarly consensus)
sources.note: Consensus: transition gradual and regionally variable, not a one-time switch or single decree.

sources.title: J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873), 'The Sabbath in the Dark Ages'
sources.note: Period source behind White: attributes suppression of the Sabbath to the Roman pontiff/papal power.

claims.quote: The disciples of Jesus are called upon to restore it, by exalting the Sabbath of the fourth commandment to its rightful position as the Creator’s memorial and the sign of His authority.
claims.claim_text: Christ's disciples are called to restore the 'seal' of God's law by exalting the seventh-day Sabbath to its rightful position.

verification.rationale: Doctrinal exhortation / prophetic application; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: “To the law and to the testimony.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the phrase "To the law and to the testimony" (cf. Isaiah 8:20).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 8:20.

claims.quote: While conflicting doctrines and theories abound, the law of God is the one unerring rule by which all opinions, doctrines, and theories are to be tested.
claims.claim_text: The law of God is the one unerring rule by which all opinions, doctrines, and theories are to be tested.

verification.rationale: Theological assertion about the authority of God's law; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: Says the prophet, “If they speak not according to this word, it is because there is no light in them.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the statement "If they speak not according to this word, it is because there is no light in them" (cf. Isaiah 8:20).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of the second half of KJV Isaiah 8:20.

claims.quote: Again, the command is given, “Cry aloud, spare not, lift up thy voice like a trumpet, and show My people their transgression, and the house of Jacob their sins.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the command "Cry aloud, spare not, lift up thy voice like a trumpet, and show My people their transgression, and the house of Jacob their sins" (cf. Isaiah 58:1).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 58:1 ("shew" modernized to "show").

claims.quote: It is not the wicked world, but those whom the Lord designates as “My people,” that are to be reproved for their transgressions.
claims.claim_text: The rebuke in Isaiah 58:1 is directed not at the wicked world in general but at those God designates as "My people."

verification.rationale: Interpretive reading of Isaiah 58; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: He declares further, “Yet they seek Me daily, and delight to know My ways, as a nation that did righteousness, and forsook not the ordinance of their God.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the statement "Yet they seek Me daily, and delight to know My ways, as a nation that did righteousness, and forsook not the ordinance of their God" (cf. Isaiah 58:2).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 58:2.

claims.quote: Here is brought to view a class who think themselves righteous, and appear to manifest great interest in the service of God; but the stern and solemn rebuke of the Searcher of hearts proves them to be trampling upon the divine precepts.
claims.claim_text: Isaiah 58:1-2 describes a class of people who consider themselves righteous but are, per the Searcher of hearts, trampling upon the divine precepts.

verification.rationale: Interpretive commentary on Isaiah 58; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: “Thou shalt raise up the foundations of many generations; and thou shalt be called, The repairer of the breach, The restorer of paths to dwell in. If thou turn away thy foot from the Sabbath, from doing thy pleasure on My holy day; and call the Sabbath a delight, the holy of the Lord, honorable; and shalt honor Him, not doing thine own ways, nor finding thine own pleasure, nor speaking thine own words: then shalt thou delight thyself in the Lord.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the passage "Thou shalt raise up the foundations of many generations... then shalt thou delight thyself in the Lord" (cf. Isaiah 58:12-14).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 58:12-14 ("honourable/honour" Americanized; "for ever" as one word). Text and sense unaltered.

claims.quote: This prophecy also applies in our time.
claims.claim_text: The Isaiah 58:12-14 prophecy of repairing the breach applies to the present (late 19th/early 20th century) time.

verification.rationale: Prophetic-application claim; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: The breach was made in the law of God when the Sabbath was changed by the Roman power.
claims.claim_text: The Roman power changed the Sabbath, thereby making a 'breach' in the law of God.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-full. Parallel to GC-26-014. Whether "the Roman power" changed the Sabbath is a genuine historiographical dispute: Bacchiocchi (Gregorian UP) assigns the Church of Rome a leadership role in promoting Sunday and degrading the Sabbath in the 2nd century; mainstream scholarship treats the change as gradual, multi-causal, and regionally variable rather than an act of "Roman power," and dual Sabbath/Sunday observance persisted for centuries. The "breach in the law" language is a theological corollary.
verification.modern_consensus: The seventh-day-to-Sunday transition was a centuries-long, uneven process; no single Roman/papal act effected it, though Rome's influence is affirmed by some scholars.

sources.title: S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday, ch.6 (Pontifical Gregorian Univ. Press, 1977)
sources.note: Church of Rome's leadership role in the 2nd-century marginalization of the Sabbath.

sources.title: When Did Sunday Become the Primary Christian Day of Worship (scholarly-consensus summary)
sources.note: Gradual, regionally variable transition; not a single decree.

sources.title: J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873)
sources.note: White's period source attributing the change to Roman/papal power.

claims.quote: But the time has come for that divine institution to be restored. The breach is to be repaired, and the foundation of many generations to be raised up.
claims.claim_text: The time has come for the seventh-day Sabbath to be restored and the 'breach' in God's law to be repaired.

verification.rationale: Prophetic-application / exhortation; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: Hallowed by the Creator’s rest and blessing, the Sabbath was kept by Adam in his innocence in holy Eden; by Adam, fallen yet repentant, when he was driven from his happy estate.
claims.claim_text: The Sabbath, hallowed by the Creator's rest and blessing, was kept by Adam both in Eden before the Fall and after his expulsion.

verification.rationale: Claim about Adam keeping the Sabbath in Eden — a theological reading of Genesis without historical/empirical evidence. Unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: It was kept by all the patriarchs, from Abel to righteous Noah, to Abraham, to Jacob.
claims.claim_text: The Sabbath was kept by all the patriarchs, including Abel, Noah, Abraham, and Jacob.

verification.rationale: Assertion that the pre-Sinai patriarchs observed the Sabbath is a theological inference debated among interpreters and unsupported by external historical evidence. Unverifiable.

claims.quote: When the chosen people were in bondage in Egypt, many, in the midst of prevailing idolatry, lost their knowledge of God’s law
claims.claim_text: During Israel's bondage in Egypt, many of the chosen people lost their knowledge of God's law amid prevailing idolatry.

verification.rationale: Theological narrative reconstruction of Israel in Egypt; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: but when the Lord delivered Israel, He proclaimed His law in awful grandeur to the assembled multitude, that they might know His will, and fear and obey Him forever.
claims.claim_text: When the Lord delivered Israel from Egypt, He proclaimed His law in grandeur to the assembled multitude so they would know, fear, and obey Him.

verification.rationale: Theological retelling of the Sinai theophany; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: From that day to the present, the knowledge of God’s law has been preserved in the earth, and the Sabbath of the fourth commandment has been kept.
claims.claim_text: From the time of the Exodus to the present, knowledge of God's law has been continuously preserved on earth and the seventh-day Sabbath has been continuously kept by some.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Read minimally ("the seventh-day Sabbath has been kept by some continuously since Sinai"), the bare continuity claim is uncontroversial: the Jewish people have observed the seventh-day Sabbath without interruption from antiquity to the present. Ball's academic study documents an additional Christian Sabbatarian strand emerging from ~1402 and flourishing after the Reformation. The stronger implication of unbroken CHRISTIAN 'faithful-remnant' observance through the medieval West is the disputed part, adjudicated separately under GC-26-032.
verification.modern_consensus: Continuous Jewish seventh-day observance is undisputed; continuous Christian seventh-day observance is not attested for the medieval Latin West.

sources.title: Review of B.W. Ball, The Seventh-Day Men (Oxford: Clarendon, 1994), Church History (Cambridge Core)
sources.note: Standard academic history of post-Reformation Sabbatarianism; first 'modern' Sabbatarian dated ~1402.

claims.quote: Though the “man of sin” succeeded in trampling under foot God’s holy day, yet even in the period of his supremacy there were, hidden in secret places, faithful souls who paid it honor.
claims.claim_text: During the period of papal ('man of sin') supremacy, faithful souls hidden in secret places continued to honor the seventh-day Sabbath.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. White's assertion of hidden medieval seventh-day Sabbath-keepers traces directly to J.N. Andrews (History of the Sabbath, 'The Sabbath in the Dark Ages'), which lists Waldenses, 'Sabbatarians in Abyssinia and Ethiopia,' Armenians, etc. as continuous observers. Modern scholarship does not substantiate continuous seventh-day (Saturday) Sabbath observance among medieval Western dissenters: the 'insabbatati' label and scattered inquisition notices are read as isolated or mislabelled, and 'history fails to substantiate the belief that the Waldenses were Seventh-Day Sabbatarians.' Ball's academic study traces the 'modern' Sabbatarian movement only to c.1402, not to a continuous medieval remnant. Wrong by modern standards but faithful to White's period source — hence anachronistic rather than simply contradicted.
verification.modern_consensus: No documented continuous seventh-day Sabbath-keeping remnant through the medieval Latin West; the Waldensian-Sabbatarian identification is unsubstantiated.
verification.period_historiography: J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873), devotes a chapter to 'The Sabbath retained through the Dark Ages by those who were not in the communion of the Romish church,' citing Waldenses, Passaginians, Ethiopians and others as seventh-day observers — the direct basis for White's statement.

sources.title: J.N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (1873), 'The Sabbath in the Dark Ages'
sources.note: White's period source asserting a hidden Sabbatarian remnant through the Dark Ages.

sources.title: Sabbath in seventh-day churches (survey of medieval Sabbatarian claims)
sources.note: Notes that the claim of Waldensian seventh-day Sabbatarianism is historically contested and largely unsubstantiated.

sources.title: Review of B.W. Ball, The Seventh-Day Men (Oxford: Clarendon, 1994)
sources.note: Traces the documented Sabbatarian movement to c.1402/post-Reformation, not a continuous medieval remnant.

claims.quote: Since the Reformation, there have been some in every generation to maintain its observance.
claims.claim_text: Since the Protestant Reformation, some people in every generation have maintained observance of the seventh-day Sabbath.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-full. Well supported. Ball's Oxford/Clarendon monograph documents a continuous English-and-Welsh Sabbatarian ('Seventh-day Men') movement from the early 17th century onward; the Mill Yard church (London, c.1650) and the Seventh Day Baptist denomination (America from 1671) provide an unbroken institutional line of seventh-day observers from the Reformation era to the present, later joined by Seventh-day Adventists. The 'some in every generation since the Reformation' claim is historically defensible.
verification.modern_consensus: A continuous Sabbatarian presence in Protestantism from the 17th century (Seventh Day Baptists) forward is well documented.

sources.title: Review of B.W. Ball, The Seventh-Day Men: Sabbatarians in England and Wales 1600-1800 (Oxford: Clarendon, 1994), Church History
sources.note: First fully documented history of the post-Reformation Sabbatarian movement.

sources.title: Seventh Day Baptists
sources.note: Mill Yard church c.1650; first American SDB church 1671; continuous denomination since.

claims.quote: Though often in the midst of reproach and persecution, a constant testimony has been borne to the perpetuity of the law of God, and the sacred obligation of the creation Sabbath.
claims.claim_text: Seventh-day Sabbath observers, often amid reproach and persecution, have continuously testified to the perpetuity of God's law and the obligation of the creation Sabbath.
claims.extractor_note: Overlaps content of GC-26-033; kept separate since it adds the specific claim of persecution.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The persecution/reproach element is supported for the post-Reformation Sabbatarians documented by Ball: the 'Seventh-day Men' faced legal penalties and social opprobrium in 17th-century England (e.g., imprisonment; Francis Bampfield died in Newgate, 1684). Their sustained polemical output ('constant testimony') to the fourth commandment's perpetuity is likewise attested. Confidence is medium because 'constant testimony ... amid persecution' is a broad characterization, strongest for the post-Reformation period and weaker for any claimed medieval continuity (see GC-26-032).

sources.title: Review of B.W. Ball, The Seventh-Day Men (Oxford: Clarendon, 1994), Church History
sources.note: Documents Sabbatarian nonconformists, their writings and the penalties they incurred.

sources.title: Seventh Day Baptists
sources.note: 17th-century Sabbatarian leaders imprisoned/penalized for their observance.

claims.quote: These truths, as presented in Revelation 14 in connection with the “everlasting gospel,” will distinguish the church of Christ at the time of His appearing.
claims.claim_text: The truths of the sanctuary and God's law, as presented in Revelation 14 with the 'everlasting gospel,' will distinguish the church of Christ at the time of His appearing.

verification.rationale: Prophetic interpretation of Revelation 14; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: For as the result of the threefold message it is announced, “Here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Revelation contains the statement "Here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus" (cf. Revelation 14:12).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of the second clause of KJV Revelation 14:12.

claims.quote: And this message is the last to be given before the coming of the Lord.
claims.claim_text: The three angels' message of Revelation 14 is the last message to be given before the second coming of Christ.

verification.rationale: Prophetic-interpretation claim; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: Immediately following its proclamation, the Son of man is seen by the prophet, coming in glory to reap the harvest of the earth.
claims.claim_text: Immediately following the proclamation of the three angels' message, Revelation depicts the Son of man coming in glory to reap the harvest of the earth.

verification.rationale: Describes the literary sequence of Revelation 14 as prophetic vision; the theological framing is not empirically checkable (the underlying textual sequence — the messages precede the 'harvest' in Rev 14:14-16 — is accurate but incidental).

claims.quote: Those who received the light concerning the sanctuary and the immutability of the law of God, were filled with joy and wonder, as they saw the beauty and harmony of the system of truth that opened to their understanding.
claims.claim_text: Early Sabbatarian Adventists who accepted the sanctuary doctrine and the immutability of God's law were filled with joy and wonder at the perceived harmony of this system of belief.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Characterization of the subjective response of the small post-1844 Sabbatarian group (Bates, the Whites, Crosier) to the new sanctuary/law synthesis. This matches the documented enthusiasm surrounding Crosier's 1845-46 sanctuary articles, out of which the Sabbatarian Adventist body cohered. A partisan self-description, but consistent with the movement's own contemporaneous accounts; not contradicted by scholarship.

sources.title: G. Land, 'The Historians and the Millerites,' AUSS 32/3 (1994)
sources.note: Historiographical survey of the post-1844 Sabbatarian development (cited as academic corroboration; PDF is an image scan).

sources.title: Millerism
sources.note: Post-1844 Sabbatarian group and its consolidation around the sanctuary doctrine.

claims.quote: They desired that the light which appeared to them so precious might be imparted to all Christians; and they could not but believe that it would be joyfully accepted.
claims.claim_text: Early Sabbatarian Adventists desired to share their sanctuary and Sabbath doctrine with all Christians, believing it would be joyfully accepted.
claims.extractor_note: Borderline: describes subjective beliefs/expectations of a historical group; included per completeness rule.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Describes the evangelistic intent of early Sabbatarian Adventists. Consistent with the group's documented efforts to publish and propagate its message (the 'Present Truth'/'Review' papers from 1849 onward). A subjective/expectational statement, but not contradicted by the historical record.

sources.title: Millerism
sources.note: Sabbatarian Adventists sought to spread their doctrine after 1844.

sources.title: G. Land, 'The Historians and the Millerites,' AUSS 32/3 (1994)
sources.note: Academic survey of the movement's post-1844 development.

claims.quote: But truths that would place them at variance with the world were not welcome to many who claimed to be followers of Christ.
claims.claim_text: Doctrines that placed believers at variance with the world were unwelcome to many who claimed to be Christians.

verification.rationale: Control-sample check of a needs_verification:false claim. The interpretive generalization carries a checkable kernel: the seventh-day Sabbath message, which set adherents at odds with the Sunday-keeping majority, met widespread resistance among professing Christians in the post-1844 milieu. That resistance is well documented (the Sabbatarian branch remained a small minority), so the kernel holds. The moral framing ('not welcome ... who claimed to be followers of Christ') remains interpretive.

sources.title: Millerism / post-1844 Sabbatarian minority
sources.note: Seventh-day Sabbath doctrine adopted by only a small fraction of Adventists; majority resisted.

claims.quote: Obedience to the fourth commandment required a sacrifice from which the majority drew back.
claims.claim_text: The majority of Adventists who encountered the Sabbath (fourth commandment) doctrine drew back from the sacrifice its observance required.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Factually the seventh-day Sabbath was accepted by only a minority of post-Disappointment Adventists; the great majority did not adopt it. 'Drew back from the sacrifice' is a value-laden framing of that documented numerical fact. The underlying claim (majority did not embrace seventh-day observance) is well supported.

sources.title: Millerism
sources.note: Sabbatarian branch was a small minority of the fragmented movement.

sources.title: Great Disappointment
sources.note: Most Adventists did not join the Sabbatarian group.

claims.quote: As the claims of the Sabbath were presented, many reasoned from the worldling’s standpoint.
claims.claim_text: When the seventh-day Sabbath doctrine was presented to Adventists, many responded by reasoning from a worldly standpoint.

verification.rationale: Interpretive characterization imputing a 'worldly' motive to those who declined the Sabbath doctrine. The bare fact of resistance is documented (see GC-26-041/042), but the motive attribution is a value judgment, not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: “We have always kept Sunday, our fathers kept it, and many good and pious men have died happy while keeping it. If they were right, so are we. The keeping of this new Sabbath would throw us out of harmony with the world, and we would have no influence over them. What can a little company keeping the seventh day hope to accomplish against all the world who are keeping Sunday?”
claims.claim_text: Opponents of the seventh-day Sabbath doctrine among 19th-century Adventists argued that Sunday-keeping was traditional, that pious forebears had died content keeping Sunday, and that adopting seventh-day observance would isolate believers from the wider world.
claims.extractor_note: This is a composite/representative quotation attributed generically to unnamed opponents ("Said they"), not a specific named individual or document. Flag for verification as characterization of period arguments rather than a locatable quote.

verification.rationale: This is a composite rhetorical quotation ('Said they'), not a verbatim citation of any named person or document — so a misquotation verdict would be inappropriate. Evaluated as a characterization of period arguments, the objections it dramatizes (Sunday as long tradition; appeal to the good deaths of Sunday-keeping forebears; fear of isolation from the wider Christian world) match the standard traditionalist case against seventh-day observance in the 19th-century Adventist debates. No evidence of fabrication; the representation is fair.

sources.title: Millerism / post-1844 Sabbath debates
sources.note: Tradition-and-majority arguments were the common objection to seventh-day observance.

claims.quote: It was by similar arguments that the Jews endeavored to justify their rejection of Christ.
claims.claim_text: Jews used arguments similar to the 'our fathers always did it this way' reasoning to justify their rejection of Christ.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Gospels repeatedly depict appeals to ancestral/traditional authority in opposition to Jesus (e.g., 'We be Abraham's seed,' John 8:33; 'the tradition of the elders,' Mark 7:3-9). White's analogy — that appeal to inherited tradition featured in first-century rejection of Christ — reflects the New Testament narrative. It is an author's comparison, but its factual kernel about tradition-based objection is supportable from the Gospel texts.

claims.quote: So, in the time of Luther, papists reasoned that true Christians had died in the Catholic faith, and therefore that religion was sufficient for salvation.
claims.claim_text: In Martin Luther's time, Catholic (papist) opponents of the Reformation argued that since true Christians had died in the Catholic faith, that faith was sufficient for salvation.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Reformation-era Catholic controversialists routinely defended the sufficiency of the received/traditional faith in which generations of Christians had lived and died, appealing to the continuity and antiquity of the Church against Protestant innovation. White's summary is a fair, generic characterization of that tradition-of-the-Church apologetic, consistent with standard accounts of Reformation polemics; it is decorative to the chapter rather than a precise citation.

claims.quote: Many urged that Sunday-keeping had been an established doctrine and a wide-spread custom of the church for many centuries.
claims.claim_text: Opponents of the seventh-day Sabbath doctrine argued that Sunday-keeping had been an established doctrine and widespread church custom for many centuries.

verification.rationale: That opponents advanced the antiquity-and-universality-of-Sunday argument is both plausible and, on its own terms, factually accurate: Sunday had indeed been the predominant Christian day of worship for many centuries. The characterization of the objection is fair and uncontested.

sources.title: Origin of Sunday observance (scholarly-consensus summary)
sources.note: Sunday widespread as the primary Christian worship day from the 2nd century onward.

claims.quote: Against this argument it was shown that the Sabbath and its observance were more ancient and widespread, even as old as the world itself, and bearing the sanction both of angels and of God.
claims.claim_text: Seventh-day Sabbatarian Adventists argued in response that Sabbath observance predates Sunday-keeping, being as old as the world and sanctioned by both angels and God.

verification.rationale: Reports the Sabbatarian counter-argument, whose content ('as old as the world,' 'sanction of angels and of God') is a theological claim about creation and is not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: When the foundations of the earth were laid, when the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy, then was laid the foundation of the Sabbath.
claims.claim_text: The Sabbath's foundation was laid at the creation of the earth, alluding to the biblical description of the morning stars singing and the sons of God shouting for joy (cf. Job 38:4-7).

verification.rationale: Theological claim about the Sabbath originating at creation, framed with an allusion to Job 38:7. Not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: Well may this institution demand our reverence: it was ordained by no human authority, and rests upon no human traditions; it was established by the Ancient of days, and commanded by His eternal word.
claims.claim_text: The Sabbath institution was ordained by God (the 'Ancient of days') rather than by human authority or tradition.

verification.rationale: Theological assertion about the divine origin of the Sabbath; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: As the attention of the people was called to the subject of Sabbath reform, popular ministers perverted the word of God, placing such interpretations upon its testimony as would best quiet inquiring minds.
claims.claim_text: When Sabbath reform was preached, popular ministers offered interpretations of scripture intended to quiet inquiring minds rather than to accurately interpret the biblical text.
claims.extractor_note: Identity of the 'popular ministers' is unspecified; general historical claim about clergy response to Sabbatarian preaching.

verification.rationale: The verifiable kernel — that mainstream clergy publicly countered Sabbatarian preaching with alternative interpretations of scripture — is documented in the antebellum Sabbath controversies. The pejorative characterization ('perverted the word of God,' 'to quiet inquiring minds') imputes bad faith and is an unverifiable value judgment, hence low confidence.

sources.title: Millerism / Sabbath controversy
sources.note: Clergy generally opposed the seventh-day Sabbath teaching.

claims.quote: And those who did not search the Scriptures for themselves were content to accept conclusions that were in accordance with their desires.
claims.claim_text: People who did not personally examine scripture were content to accept clergy conclusions matching their own preferences.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/moral generalization about laypeople's motives; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: By argument, sophistry, the traditions of the Fathers, and the authority of the church, many endeavored to overthrow the truth.
claims.claim_text: Opponents of the Sabbatarian Adventist message used argument, sophistry, patristic tradition, and church authority to try to overturn its claims.

verification.rationale: The kernel — that opponents appealed to patristic tradition and church authority in arguing against seventh-day observance — is consistent with the anti-Sabbatarian case, which characteristically invoked the practice of the early church and long ecclesiastical custom. 'Sophistry' is a partisan characterization. Low confidence given the generic, unattributed framing.

sources.title: Origin of Sunday observance (scholarly summary)
sources.note: Appeals to church tradition/antiquity were the standard defense of Sunday.

claims.quote: Its advocates were driven to their Bibles to defend the validity of the fourth commandment.
claims.claim_text: Advocates of the seventh-day Sabbath doctrine relied on scripture alone to defend the validity of the fourth commandment.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the documented sola-scriptura method of early Sabbatarian Adventist controversialists (e.g., Joseph Bates, J.N. Andrews), who grounded the seventh-day argument in biblical exegesis of the fourth commandment. Fair and uncontroversial characterization.

sources.title: Millerism / Sabbatarian Adventism
sources.note: Early Sabbatarian Adventists argued the Sabbath from scripture.

claims.quote: Humble men, armed with the Word of truth alone, withstood the attacks of men of learning, who, with surprise and anger, found their eloquent sophistry powerless against the simple, straightforward reasoning of men who were versed in the Scriptures rather than in the subtleties of the schools.
claims.claim_text: Unlearned advocates of the Sabbatarian Adventist message, using scripture alone, successfully withstood the arguments of formally educated opponents.
claims.extractor_note: Identities of both groups are unspecified; general historical/biographical claim about early Sabbatarian Adventist controversialists.

verification.rationale: The kernel — that largely self-taught lay Sabbatarian debaters engaged trained clergy in public disputation — is consistent with the movement's history (many early Adventist leaders lacked formal theological training). The claimed OUTCOME ('withstood,' opponents' 'sophistry powerless') is a partisan verdict on debates that has no neutral scorekeeping, so confidence is low.

sources.title: Millerism / early Adventist debaters
sources.note: Early Sabbatarian Adventist leaders were mostly without formal academic training.

claims.quote: “Why do not our great men understand this Sabbath question? But few believe as you do. It cannot be that you are right, and that all the men of learning in the world are wrong.”
claims.claim_text: Opponents of the seventh-day Sabbath doctrine argued that since few people and few learned men accepted it, it could not be correct.
claims.extractor_note: Composite/representative quotation attributed generically to unnamed opponents ("many... urged"), not a specific named individual or document.

verification.rationale: A composite representative quotation, not a verbatim citation, so no misquotation issue arises. As a characterization of period argument, the appeal-to-authority/appeal-to-majority objection ('the learned reject it, so it must be wrong') is a natural and well-attested line against a small dissenting group; the depiction is fair and unfabricated.

sources.title: Millerism / Sabbatarian minority
sources.note: Seventh-day view held by only a small minority, inviting appeal-to-majority objections.

claims.quote: The reason why He does not oftener choose men of learning and high position to lead out in reform movements, is that they trust to their creeds, theories, and theological systems, and feel no need to be taught of God.
claims.claim_text: God does not usually choose learned and high-position men to lead reform movements because such men trust their own creeds and theological systems rather than seeking to be taught of God.

verification.rationale: Theological claim about divine selection and human motives; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: Only those who have a personal connection with the Source of wisdom are able to understand or explain the Scriptures.
claims.claim_text: Only those with a personal connection to God are able to properly understand or explain scripture.

verification.rationale: Theological assertion about spiritual illumination and scripture; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: The majority of Adventists rejected the truths concerning the sanctuary and the law of God, and many also renounced their faith in the Advent Movement, and adopted unsound and conflicting views of the prophecies which applied to that work.
claims.claim_text: After the Great Disappointment, the majority of Adventists rejected the sanctuary doctrine and the seventh-day law message, and many renounced their faith in the Advent Movement altogether, adopting varied and conflicting prophetic interpretations.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Well supported by Millerite historiography: after 22 Oct 1844 the once-united movement fragmented, most did not accept the Sabbatarian sanctuary/law synthesis (a minority position), and a proliferation of conflicting explanations and re-interpretations of the prophecies ensued, with many abandoning the movement entirely.
verification.modern_consensus: The post-Disappointment movement fractured into multiple groups with divergent prophetic views; the Sabbatarian sanctuary branch was a minority.

sources.title: Great Disappointment
sources.note: Post-1844 fragmentation; many gave up their beliefs, others advanced conflicting explanations.

sources.title: G. Land, 'The Historians and the Millerites,' AUSS 32/3 (1994)
sources.note: Academic survey of post-Disappointment fragmentation.

claims.quote: Some were led into the error of repeatedly fixing upon a definite time for the coming of Christ.
claims.claim_text: Some Adventists, after 1844, repeatedly set new definite dates for the second coming of Christ.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Documented: after October 1844 various Millerites set successive new dates (April, July, and October 1845, and later), a recurring feature of the movement's fragmentation.

sources.title: Great Disappointment
sources.note: New dates predicted after 1844 (April, July, October 1845, etc.).

sources.title: Millerism
sources.note: Repeated post-1844 date-setting among factions.

claims.quote: The light which was now shining on the subject of the sanctuary would have shown them that no prophetic period extends to the second advent; that the exact time of this event is not foretold.
claims.claim_text: The sanctuary doctrine, properly understood, shows that no prophetic period extends to the second advent and that its exact time is not foretold in scripture.

verification.rationale: Doctrinal claim about the interpretation of prophetic periods; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: But, turning from the light, they continued to set time after time for the Lord to come, and as often they were disappointed.
claims.claim_text: Certain Adventists continued repeatedly to set new dates for Christ's return after 1844, and were repeatedly disappointed.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Same well-documented pattern as GC-26-060: successive post-1844 date predictions, each followed by disappointment, are attested in Millerite history.

sources.title: Great Disappointment
sources.note: Successive failed dates after 1844.

sources.title: Millerism
sources.note: Repeated date-setting and disappointment among factions.

claims.quote: When the Thessalonian church received erroneous views concerning the coming of Christ, the apostle Paul counseled them to test their hopes and anticipations carefully by the word of God.
claims.claim_text: The apostle Paul counseled the church at Thessalonica, which had adopted erroneous views about the timing of Christ's coming, to test their expectations by scripture.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurately summarizes 2 Thessalonians 2:1-3, where Paul corrects the Thessalonians' belief that 'the day of Christ is at hand' and urges them not to be shaken or deceived. Standard content of the epistle.

claims.quote: He cited them to prophecies revealing the events to take place before Christ should come, and showed that they had no ground to expect Him in their day.
claims.claim_text: Paul cited the Thessalonians to prophecies of events that must occur before Christ's coming, showing they had no scriptural ground to expect Him in their own lifetime.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Reflects 2 Thessalonians 2:3, where Paul states that the day will not come 'except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed' — i.e., prerequisite events must precede Christ's coming. Accurate summary of the text.

claims.quote: “Let no man deceive you by any means,”
claims.claim_text: The apostle Paul wrote "Let no man deceive you by any means" in warning the Thessalonians about the timing of Christ's coming (cf. 2 Thessalonians 2:3).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of the opening of KJV 2 Thessalonians 2:3, correctly contextualized.

claims.quote: Many Adventists have felt that unless they could fix their faith upon a definite time for the Lord’s coming, they could not be zealous and diligent in the work of preparation.
claims.claim_text: Many Adventists believed that fixing a definite date for Christ's return was necessary in order to remain zealous and diligent in preparation.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The definite-time expectation was central to Millerite motivation; the persistent date-setting after 1844 reflects exactly this felt need for a fixed date to sustain urgency. A reasonable characterization of the movement's psychology, consistent with its history.

sources.title: Millerism
sources.note: Definite-time expectancy drove Millerite zeal and post-1844 date-setting.

claims.quote: But as their hopes are again and again excited, only to be destroyed, their faith receives such a shock that it becomes well-nigh impossible for them to be impressed by the great truths of prophecy.
claims.claim_text: Repeated raising and dashing of date-setting expectations among Adventists caused a shock to their faith that made them resistant to prophetic teaching thereafter.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The general pattern — that repeated failed predictions produced disillusionment and loss of confidence in prophetic teaching — is broadly consistent with the documented collapse of confidence after the Great Disappointment and later failed dates. The specific psychological causal mechanism is a plausible generalization rather than a precisely measurable historical fact, hence low confidence.

sources.title: Great Disappointment
sources.note: Failed predictions produced widespread disillusionment and loss of momentum.

claims.quote: The preaching of a definite time for the judgment, in the giving of the first message, was ordered of God.
claims.claim_text: The preaching of a definite time for the judgment in the first angel's message (Millerite movement) was ordered by God.

verification.rationale: Theological claim of divine ordination of the Millerite time-message; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: The computation of the prophetic periods on which that message was based, placing the close of the 2300 days in the autumn of 1844, stands without impeachment.
claims.claim_text: The Millerite computation of prophetic periods, placing the close of the 2300-day prophecy in the autumn of 1844, is accurate and unrefuted.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial (relying on the well-established critical consensus reported in standard Daniel commentaries, e.g. J.J. Collins, Daniel, Hermeneia). The claim that the 2300-days=1844 computation 'stands without impeachment' is far stronger than merely reporting the calculation, and it is not sustained. Mainstream OT scholarship dates Daniel to the 2nd century BC and reads the '2300 evenings and mornings' (Dan 8:14) as the Antiochene desecration of the temple (c.167-164 BC), commonly ~1150 days, rejecting the year-day principle that converts the figure to years. Even within the year-day framework the anchor date is contested: the mainstream date for Artaxerxes' decree/Ezra's return is 458 BC, not the 457 BC Miller required, and Daniel 8's 2300 days are not textually linked to Daniel 9's 70 weeks. Consistent with this project's treatment of the 457-BC anchor in chs. 18/22/23, the underlying calculation is a genuine, ongoing SDA-vs-mainstream dispute; White's 'without impeachment' assertion overstates it, but the matter turns on contested interpretive premises rather than a single demonstrable factual error, so 'disputed' is the calibrated verdict.
verification.modern_consensus: Critical scholarship reads Dan 8:14 as referring to Antiochus IV (2nd c. BC), rejects the year-day principle, and does not recognize any prophetic period terminating in 1844; the 457-vs-458 BC anchor is itself contested.

sources.title: Great Disappointment (Miller's 2300-day/457 BC computation and its critique)
sources.note: Documents the 457 BC + year-day basis and that the two time-prophecies (Dan 8 and Dan 9) were linked by Miller.

sources.title: Understanding the 2,300 'Evenings and Mornings' of Daniel 8:14 (summarizing the dominant Antiochus/Maccabean reading it contests)
sources.note: Cited as a position: acknowledges that 'the vast majority of interpreters' identify Dan 8 with Antiochus IV and construe 2300 evening-mornings as ~1150 days, against the year-day/1844 reading.

claims.quote: The repeated efforts to find new dates for the beginning and close of the prophetic periods, and the unsound reasoning necessary to sustain these positions, not only lead minds away from the present truth, but throw contempt upon all efforts to explain the prophecies.
claims.claim_text: Repeated post-1844 efforts by Adventists to set new prophetic dates, and the unsound reasoning required to sustain them, led minds away from the sanctuary message and brought contempt on prophetic interpretation generally.

verification.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The factual core — repeated post-1844 date-setting occurred and discredited prophetic date-preaching in the eyes of many — is consistent with the historiography of the movement's decline in credibility after successive failures. The internal-theology framing ('present truth,' 'unsound reasoning') is denominational; the historical kernel is supportable.

sources.title: Great Disappointment
sources.note: Repeated failed dates brought ridicule on the movement's prophetic claims.

claims.quote: The more frequently a definite time is set for the second advent, and the more widely it is taught, the better it suits the purposes of Satan.
claims.claim_text: Frequent, widely taught date-setting for the second advent serves Satan's purposes.

verification.rationale: Theological claim invoking Satanic agency; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: After the time has passed, he excites ridicule and contempt of its advocates, and thus casts reproach upon the great Advent Movement of 1843 and 1844.
claims.claim_text: The Advent Movement spanned 1843 and 1844, and subsequent failed date-setting brought ridicule and reproach upon it.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Millerite Advent Movement's climactic expectation spanned 1843 (Miller's original 'about 1843' window) into 1844 (the 22 October 'seventh-month' date), and the Great Disappointment brought widespread public ridicule upon the movement. Textbook Millerite chronology.

claims.quote: Those who persist in this error will at last fix upon a date too far in the future for the coming of Christ. Thus they will be led to rest in a false security, and many will not be undeceived until it is too late.
claims.claim_text: Those who persist in setting dates for Christ's second coming will eventually fix on a date so far in the future that it produces false security, undeceiving many too late.

verification.rationale: Predictive/theological warning; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: The history of ancient Israel is a striking illustration of the past experience of the Adventist body.
claims.claim_text: The history of ancient Israel is a striking illustration of the Adventist movement's past experience.

verification.rationale: Interpretive typological analogy between Israel and the Adventist movement; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: God led His people in the Advent Movement, even as He led the children of Israel from Egypt.
claims.claim_text: God led His people in the Advent Movement in the same manner He led the children of Israel out of Egypt.

verification.rationale: Theological claim of divine guidance; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: In the great disappointment their faith was tested as was that of the Hebrews at the Red Sea.
claims.claim_text: The faith of Adventists in the 1844 Great Disappointment was tested in a manner parallel to the Hebrews' test of faith at the Red Sea.

verification.rationale: The historical kernel — that the Great Disappointment of 22 October 1844 was a severe test of faith for the Millerites — is well documented. The comparison to the Hebrews at the Red Sea is an interpretive typology, not itself a factual claim. On its verifiable content (the Disappointment as a profound trial of faith), it is supported.

sources.title: Great Disappointment
sources.note: Oct 22, 1844 remembered as a profound crisis/test of faith for believers.

claims.quote: If all who had labored unitedly in the work in 1844, had received the third angel’s message and proclaimed it in the power of the Holy Spirit, the Lord would have wrought mightily with their efforts.
claims.claim_text: Had all who labored unitedly in the 1844 Advent movement accepted and proclaimed the third angel's message in the Holy Spirit's power, God would have worked mightily with their efforts.

verification.rationale: Counterfactual theological claim about divine action; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: A flood of light would have been shed upon the world. Years ago the inhabitants of the earth would have been warned, the closing work completed, and Christ would have come for the redemption of His people.
claims.claim_text: Had the 1844 Adventists proclaimed the third angel's message unitedly, the world would already have been warned, the closing work completed, and Christ would already have come.

verification.rationale: Counterfactual eschatological claim; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: It was not the will of God that Israel should wander forty years in the wilderness
claims.claim_text: The Israelites wandered forty years in the wilderness after the Exodus.
claims.extractor_note: Underlying biblical chronological claim (cf. Numbers 14:33-34), embedded in a statement about divine will.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] The forty-year wilderness wandering is the explicit biblical datum (Numbers 14:33-34; Deuteronomy 8:2). Reported here as the content of the biblical narrative; the surrounding statement about God's will is theological.

claims.quote: It was not the will of God that Israel should wander forty years in the wilderness; He desired to lead them directly to the land of Canaan, and establish them there, a holy, happy people.
claims.claim_text: It was not God's original will that Israel wander forty years in the wilderness; He intended to lead them directly to Canaan.

verification.rationale: Theological claim about God's original intention; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: But “they could not enter in because of unbelief.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Hebrews states of the wilderness generation of Israel, "they could not enter in because of unbelief" (cf. Hebrews 3:19).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of the end of KJV Hebrews 3:19 ("So we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief").

claims.quote: Because of their backsliding and apostasy, they perished in the desert, and others were raised up to enter the promised land.
claims.claim_text: Because of backsliding and apostasy, the Exodus generation of Israel perished in the wilderness, and a subsequent generation was raised up to enter the promised land.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate summary of the biblical narrative (Numbers 14; 26:64-65; Joshua): the Exodus generation died in the wilderness and their children entered Canaan. Reported as the content of the text.

claims.quote: In like manner, it was not the will of God that the coming of Christ should be so long delayed, and His people should remain so many years in this world of sin and sorrow.
claims.claim_text: It was not God's original will that Christ's second coming be delayed so long past 1844, keeping His people in this world of sin and sorrow.

verification.rationale: Theological claim about God's will regarding the timing of the Second Advent; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: But unbelief separated them from God. As they refused to do the work which He had appointed them, others were raised up to proclaim the message.
claims.claim_text: Unbelief among post-1844 Adventists separated them from God, and because they refused to do the appointed work, others were raised up to proclaim the message.

verification.rationale: Theological interpretation of post-1844 history in terms of divine appointment and unbelief; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: In mercy to the world, Jesus delays His coming, that sinners may have an opportunity to hear the warning, and find in Him a shelter before the wrath of God shall be poured out.
claims.claim_text: Jesus delays His second coming in mercy so that sinners may have opportunity to hear the warning message and find shelter before God's wrath is poured out.

verification.rationale: Theological claim about divine motive for delaying the Second Advent; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: Now, as in former ages, the presentation of a truth that reproves the sins and errors of the times, will excite opposition.
claims.claim_text: The presentation of truth that reproves the sins and errors of the age excites opposition, now as in former ages.

verification.rationale: Interpretive generalization about the reception of reform; not a discrete empirically checkable claim.

claims.quote: “Every one that doeth evil hateth the light, neither cometh to the light, lest his deeds should be reproved.”
claims.claim_text: The Gospel of John contains the statement "Every one that doeth evil hateth the light, neither cometh to the light, lest his deeds should be reproved" (cf. John 3:20).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV John 3:20 (opening "For" dropped).

claims.quote: Elijah was declared to be a troubler in Israel
claims.claim_text: The prophet Elijah was declared to be a 'troubler in Israel' (cf. 1 Kings 18:17).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Reflects 1 Kings 18:17, where Ahab greets Elijah, 'Art thou he that troubleth Israel?' Accurate summary of the text.

claims.quote: Jeremiah a traitor
claims.claim_text: The prophet Jeremiah was declared to be a traitor (cf. Jeremiah 37-38).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] In Jeremiah 37-38 the prophet is accused of deserting/falling away to the Chaldeans (Jer 37:13-14) and of weakening the defenders' morale, and is imprisoned as a traitor. Accurate summary of the narrative.

claims.quote: Paul a polluter of the temple.
claims.claim_text: The apostle Paul was accused of being a polluter of the temple (cf. Acts 21:28).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Reflects Acts 21:28, where the crowd charges that Paul 'hath polluted this holy place' by supposedly bringing Greeks into the temple. Accurate summary of the text.

claims.quote: From that day to this, those who would be loyal to truth have been denounced as seditious, heretical, or schismatic.
claims.claim_text: Those loyal to truth have historically been denounced as seditious, heretical, or schismatic.

verification.rationale: Control-sample check of a needs_verification:false claim. The sweeping generalization rests on a checkable historical kernel: religious dissenters and reformers were indeed routinely labeled seditious, heretical, or schismatic (e.g., Jan Hus condemned as a heretic at Constance; Luther declared a heretic and outlaw; countless nonconformists prosecuted for schism/sedition). The pattern the sentence asserts is well attested; the value term 'loyal to truth' is the author's framing.

sources.title: General church history of heresy/schism accusations against reformers (illustrative)
sources.note: Reformers such as Hus and Luther were formally denounced as heretics/schismatics.

claims.quote: This spirit will increase more and more.
claims.claim_text: The spirit of opposition to reproof of popular sins will increase more and more in the future.

verification.rationale: Predictive/prophetic claim about the future; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: And the Bible plainly teaches that a time is approaching when the laws of the state will so conflict with the law of God that whosoever would obey all the divine precepts must brave reproach and punishment as an evil-doer.
claims.claim_text: The Bible teaches that a future time is approaching when state law will conflict with God's law such that obeying divine precepts will bring reproach and punishment.

verification.rationale: Prophetic-interpretation claim about a future church-state conflict; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: The confession of faith made by saints and martyrs was recorded for the benefit of succeeding generations.
claims.claim_text: The confessions of faith made by saints and martyrs were recorded for the benefit of succeeding generations.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/homiletical statement about the purpose of martyrological records; not a discrete empirically checkable claim.

claims.quote: Anciently the Lord declared to one who spoke in His name, “The house of Israel will not hearken unto thee; for they will not hearken unto Me.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Ezekiel contains the Lord's declaration to Ezekiel, "The house of Israel will not hearken unto thee; for they will not hearken unto Me" (cf. Ezekiel 3:7).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Ezekiel 3:7 (opening "But" dropped as the quote begins mid-verse).

claims.quote: Nevertheless He said, “Thou shalt speak My words unto them, whether they will hear, or whether they will forbear.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Ezekiel contains the Lord's instruction, "Thou shalt speak My words unto them, whether they will hear, or whether they will forbear" (cf. Ezekiel 2:7).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Ezekiel 2:7 (opening "And" dropped).

claims.quote: “Son of man, I have set thee a watchman unto the house of Israel; therefore thou shalt hear the word at My mouth, and warn them from Me. When I say unto the wicked, O wicked man, thou shalt surely die; if thou dost not speak to warn the wicked from his way, that wicked man shall die in his iniquity; but his blood will I require at thine hand. Nevertheless, if thou warn the wicked of his way to turn from it; if he do not turn from his way, he shall die in his iniquity; but thou hast delivered thy soul.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Ezekiel records God appointing Ezekiel as a 'watchman' to the house of Israel, charged to warn the wicked or bear responsibility for their blood (cf. Ezekiel 33:7-9).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Ezekiel 33:7-9 (opening "So thou, O" dropped as the quote begins mid-verse). Text and sense unaltered.

claims.quote: The great obstacle both to the acceptance and to the promulgation of truth, is the fact that it involves inconvenience and reproach.
claims.claim_text: The greatest obstacle to accepting and spreading truth is that it involves inconvenience and reproach.

verification.rationale: Interpretive generalization about human motives and truth; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: with the apostle Paul counting that “our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory;”
claims.claim_text: The apostle Paul wrote that "our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory" (cf. 2 Corinthians 4:17).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV 2 Corinthians 4:17 (opening "For" dropped), correctly attributed to Paul.

claims.quote: with one of old, “esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures in Egypt.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Hebrews describes Moses as "esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures in Egypt" (cf. Hebrews 11:26).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Hebrews 11:26, which in context (Heb 11:24-26) refers to Moses. Accurate quotation and attribution.

claims.quote: To men of principle, faith, and daring, the world is indebted for its great reforms.
claims.claim_text: The world's great reforms are owed to men of principle, faith, and daring.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/rhetorical generalization about the sources of historical reform; not a discrete empirically checkable claim.

claims.quote: “Hearken unto Me, ye that know righteousness, the people in whose heart is My law; fear ye not the reproach of men, neither be ye afraid of their revilings. For the moth shall eat them up like a garment, and the worm shall eat them like wool: but My righteousness shall be forever, and My salvation from generation to generation.”
claims.claim_text: The book of Isaiah contains the passage "Hearken unto Me, ye that know righteousness... but My righteousness shall be forever, and My salvation from generation to generation" (cf. Isaiah 51:7-8).

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Verbatim quotation of KJV Isaiah 51:7-8 ("for ever" rendered "forever"). Text and sense unaltered.
